Annual Dispatch — grid_beta

Loading…
copperplate no redispatch no line constraints MILP unit-commitment v5-calibrated merit order

The grid_beta scenario was dispatched for all 8,760 hours of 2025 using the calibrated MILP unit-commitment merit order. Lines and transformers are present in the model but no line capacity constraints are enforced — this is a single-zone copperplate dispatch. No second-stage redispatch is applied.

Annual Energy Mix

Monthly Generation Stack

Comparison vs. Energy-Charts (when available)

Annual TWh per fuel category as observed by Energy-Charts (real DE 2025) versus the model. The merit order was calibrated against this data so most fuel categories match within ±10%.

Full-Year Hourly Dispatch (8,760 h)

Stacked-area view of dispatched generation by carrier, with the clearing price overlaid (right axis). Use the range-slider beneath the chart to zoom into specific weeks or months. All 8,760 hourly values are embedded in this page.

Dispatch + Demand

Total dispatched generation vs. demand. The slight gap is filled by storage and net imports.

Load Duration Curve

All 8,760 hourly demand values, sorted descending. The leftmost edge is peak demand; the area under the curve is total annual electricity consumption.

Residual Load (Demand − Variable RES)

Demand minus solar + onshore + offshore wind dispatch. Negative values indicate hours where instantaneous variable-RES generation exceeded demand — the surplus is exported, charged into pumped storage, or curtailed.

Price Duration Curve

All 8,760 hourly clearing prices, sorted descending. Negative-price hours occur during high RES + low residual load.

Annual Net Imports per Border

Net imports from each interconnected country. Positive = into Germany. Calibrated against ENTSO-E cross-border physical flows for 2025 (annual: 76.2 TWh imports, 54.3 TWh exports, net importer in 2025).

Hourly Net Imports (8,760 h)

Pumped-Storage Cycling

Aggregate pumped-storage discharge across all PSP units (9.9 GW DE fleet including coupled Vianden plant in Luxembourg). Charge is approximated from energy balance with a 75% round-trip efficiency.

State of Charge (estimated)

SoC is reconstructed from the discharge profile and the round-trip efficiency assumption. Only the discharge leg comes directly from the MILP; the charge leg is allocated proportionally to off-peak hours.

Top 50 Generators by Annual Output

Ranked by total dispatched MWh. Click a column header to re-sort.

Methodology

Scenario

This dispatch runs on grid_beta, the 2025-calibrated full-resolution version of the eGon German transmission grid. Topology: — buses, — AC lines, — generators (MaStR-derived), PSP+battery storage units, and 11 cross-border interconnectors with NTCs calibrated from 2025 ENTSO-E physical flows.

Dispatch model

The dispatch is produced by scripts/simulation/merit_order_comparison.py's run_milp_uc() — a MILP unit-commitment heuristic with rolling 48-hour horizon and 24-hour stride. Thermal plants (coal, lignite, gas CCGT, gas CHP) carry min-up and min-down constraints; CHP must-run is treated as price-taker generation; hydro reservoir, biomass and waste are handled as must-run with seasonal availability; pumped storage cycles intra-window with a 50-GWh fleet energy capacity.

What "no line constraints" means

The full grid_beta topology (lines, transformers, HVDC links) is loaded into the saved PyPSA network so the file can be used for downstream power-flow analysis, but no line capacity is enforced during dispatch. This is implemented by running the MILP on a single-zone copperplate model and then mapping the fuel-level hourly dispatch back onto each individual generator on the topology, weighted by p_max_pu × p_nom at every hour. The clearing price is therefore uniform across the entire German bidding zone (one number per hour), which is a faithful representation of the actual day-ahead market design — the only departure from reality is the absence of intraday redispatch.

Calibration

The merit-order parameters were calibrated against EnergyCharts and SMARD 2025 data so that all 13 of {imports, exports, mean price, solar, onshore wind, offshore wind, biomass, hydro, pumped storage, gas, hard coal, lignite, other-conventional} match real DE 2025 within ±10 %. The worst gap is +9.4 % on imports; six metrics match within ±1 %. EnergyCharts public_power undercounts behind-the-meter generation by ~70 TWh, so the comparison includes display-side BTM corrections of +35 TWh on gas (industrial CHP), +30 TWh on solar (rooftop self-consumption) and +5 TWh on biomass.

Caveats

  • Copperplate price. Real day-ahead markets do experience zonal congestion; in this run all internal congestion is implicitly handled by an unconstrained TSO.
  • No line losses are modelled (would be ≈3% on AC + 2% on HVDC).
  • Battery storage (2,416 units, 2.6 GW) is left at zero in this run because the MILP only tracks PSP. Future work should add an LP layer for utility batteries.
  • SMARD load vs grid_beta load. The dispatch is balanced against SMARD's actual 2025 demand (462 TWh). The grid_beta BDEW load of 448 TWh is preserved on per-load time-series for spatial analysis but is not the demand the MILP is solving for.
  • Per-generator dispatch is allocated, not solved. Within each carrier, the fuel-level MILP output is distributed across individual MaStR units proportionally to their availability. Two coal plants of equal size at the same hour will get the same dispatch — this is fine for energy aggregates but not for plant-level decisions.

What changes if line constraints are turned on?

Some areas would become congested (notably north–south wind transit), meaning more dispatch from southern gas plants and curtailment of northern wind. Real-world redispatch volumes in 2024 were ~28 TWh (BNetzA), so the unconstrained dispatch under-counts thermal generation by roughly that amount.

Generated — from results/dispatch_8760h.nc.
